class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Business Law and the Legal System
learning_objectives: Describe the sources of American law: constitutional, statutory, regulatory, case law
Explain the court system structure and jurisdiction
Distinguish between civil and criminal liability in business

lecture_notes.introduction: Business law provides the framework within which all commercial activity occurs. Understanding legal principles helps managers avoid costly mistakes and make better decisions.
key_concepts: Sources of law: Constitution, statutes, regulations, common law
Federal vs. state court systems
Civil law: contracts, torts, property vs. criminal law
Alternative dispute resolution: mediation, arbitration
The litigation process: complaint through appeal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case briefing exercise: analyzing a landmark business law case using IRAC method (Issue, Rule, Application, Conclusion)
setup_requirements: Legal case database access (LexisNexis or Westlaw)

review.value: Issue (what is the legal question), Rule (what law applies), Application (how does the law apply to these facts), Conclusion (what is the outcome)
Courts follow precedent: similar cases should be decided similarly, providing predictability in the legal system

learning_objectives: Identify the elements required for a valid contract
Distinguish between void, voidable, and unenforceable contracts
Analyze common breach of contract remedies
readings: UCC Article 2 Summary for Business Students

lecture_notes.introduction: Contracts are the foundation of all business relationships. Every purchase order, employment agreement, lease, and partnership depends on enforceable contracts.
key_concepts: Elements: offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, legality
Express vs. implied contracts
Statute of Frauds: when written contracts are required
Breach remedies: compensatory, consequential, liquidated damages
UCC vs. common law: sale of goods vs. services
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract analysis exercise: identifying formation issues and potential breaches in sample business agreements

review.value: Something of value exchanged between parties; a promise without consideration is generally unenforceable (a gift, not a contract)
Certain contracts must be in writing: land sales, contracts over $500 for goods, contracts not performable within one year, suretyships

learning_objectives: Compare legal structures: sole proprietorship, partnership, LLC, corporation
Explain the duties and liabilities of corporate directors and officers

lecture_notes.introduction: The legal form of a business determines liability exposure, tax treatment, management structure, and ability to raise capital. Corporate governance ensures accountability to shareholders.
key_concepts: Sole proprietorship: simplicity but unlimited liability
Partnership: general vs. limited; partnership agreements
LLC: liability protection with pass-through taxation
Corporation: separate legal entity, double taxation, perpetual existence
Fiduciary duties: duty of care, duty of loyalty, business judgment rule
lecture_notes.demonstration: Entity selection exercise: recommending the optimal legal structure for three different business scenarios

review.value: Owners' personal assets are protected from business debts; available in LLCs and corporations but not sole proprietorships or general partnerships
Courts will not second-guess directors' decisions made in good faith, with due care, and without conflicts of interest

class_schedule.topic: Intellectual Property and Digital Business Law
learning_objectives: Distinguish between patents, trademarks, copyrights, and trade secrets
Analyze legal issues in e-commerce and digital privacy
Evaluate the impact of technology on business law
readings: GDPR and CCPA Overview for Business

lecture_notes.introduction: In a knowledge economy, intellectual property is often a company's most valuable asset. Digital business creates new legal challenges around privacy, cybersecurity, and jurisdiction.
key_concepts: Patents: utility, design, plant; 20-year protection
Trademarks: brand identity protection; registration and infringement
Copyright: original works of authorship; fair use doctrine
Trade secrets: protection through confidentiality, not registration
Data privacy laws: GDPR, CCPA, and emerging regulations
lecture_notes.demonstration: IP audit exercise: identifying and categorizing a tech startup's intellectual property assets and recommending protection strategies
setup_requirements: Sample NDA and IP assignment agreements

review.value: Patents provide strong but time-limited protection (20 years) with public disclosure; trade secrets offer unlimited duration but no protection if independently discovered
Permits limited use of copyrighted material for criticism, commentary, education, or parody; four-factor balancing test

class_schedule.topic: Ethical Frameworks and Corporate Social Responsibility
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks: utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics, social contract
Evaluate stakeholder theory vs. shareholder primacy
Analyze CSR initiatives for authenticity and impact
readings: Friedman, The Social Responsibility of Business (NYT, 1970)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethics goes beyond legal compliance. Many actions are legal but unethical, and ethical decision-making requires frameworks for reasoning through complex dilemmas.
key_concepts: Utilitarianism: greatest good for the greatest number
Deontological ethics: duty-based, certain acts are inherently right or wrong
Virtue ethics: character and moral excellence
Stakeholder theory: considering all affected parties
ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) and sustainable business
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical dilemma analysis: a pharmaceutical company must decide whether to raise the price of a life-saving drug, applying each ethical framework

review.value: Shareholder primacy (Friedman): the only social responsibility is to increase profits. Stakeholder theory (Freeman): the firm must consider employees, customers, communities, and the environment
Environmental, Social, Governance criteria used by investors to evaluate corporate sustainability and ethical impact beyond financial performance

class_schedule.topic: Employment Law, Antitrust, and Regulatory Compliance
learning_objectives: Identify key employment law protections and compliance requirements
Explain antitrust law and its application to business conduct
Design a compliance program for a business organization

lecture_notes.introduction: Regulatory compliance is not optional. The legal landscape includes employment protections, antitrust rules, environmental regulations, and industry-specific requirements that every manager must understand.
key_concepts: Employment law: Title VII, ADA, OSHA, workers' compensation
Sherman Act: price fixing, market allocation, monopolization
Clayton Act: mergers, exclusive dealing, price discrimination
Environmental regulations: Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act
Compliance programs: elements of an effective program
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compliance scenario analysis: identifying legal violations and designing corrective actions for a company facing regulatory scrutiny

review.key: Per Se vs. Rule of Reason
review.value: Per se violations (price fixing) are automatically illegal. Rule of reason requires showing the conduct unreasonably restrains trade.
Written standards, designated officer, training, monitoring, reporting mechanisms, enforcement, response protocols

questions.question: A 16-year-old signs a contract to purchase a $3,000 laptop for their business. Three weeks later, they want to return it. Can they void the contract? Explain using contract law principles.
questions.answer: Yes, a minor (under 18) has the right to disaffirm (void) a contract because they lack legal capacity. The contract is voidable at the minor's option. The minor can return the laptop and recover the purchase price. However, some jurisdictions require the minor to return the item if still in their possession, and the minor may be liable for the reasonable value of any benefit received (the use of the laptop for three weeks). The adult party cannot void the contract; only the minor has that right.

rubric.key: Explains voidable nature and minor's right to disaffirm

questions.question: Three friends want to start a restaurant together. One will invest $100,000 but not work in the business. The others will manage daily operations. Recommend a business entity and explain why.
questions.answer: An LLC (Limited Liability Company) is the best recommendation. It provides all three members with limited liability protection (protecting personal assets from restaurant liabilities, which are significant in the food industry). It offers pass-through taxation (avoiding double taxation of a corporation). The operating agreement can specify that the passive investor has different rights than the managing members. Unlike a limited partnership, all members get liability protection regardless of their involvement level.

rubric.key: Addresses liability protection for all members
Explains why alternatives are less suitable

questions.question: A former employee of Company A joins competitor Company B and uses a proprietary customer database from memory (not copied files). Has the former employee violated trade secret law? Analyze.
questions.answer: This depends on several factors. If Company A took reasonable steps to protect the information as a trade secret (NDAs, restricted access, marking as confidential) and the database information qualifies as a trade secret (provides competitive value and is not generally known), then using it from memory likely constitutes misappropriation under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The fact that it was memorized rather than copied does not create an exception; the acquisition through a position of trust and subsequent disclosure or use without authorization is the violation. However, general skills and knowledge (non-proprietary) that the employee developed are not protected.

rubric.key: Addresses memory vs. copying distinction correctly
Notes dependence on company's protection measures

questions.question: Using two different ethical frameworks, analyze whether a social media company should sell user data to third-party advertisers without explicit opt-in consent.
questions.answer: Utilitarian analysis: Must weigh the benefit to the company (revenue) and advertisers (targeted marketing) against the harm to users (privacy violation, potential data misuse, loss of trust). Since millions of users are affected and privacy violations can cause significant harm (identity theft, manipulation, discrimination), the aggregate harm likely outweighs the benefits, making the practice unethical. Deontological analysis: Users have a right to privacy and autonomy over their personal information. Selling data without explicit consent treats users merely as means to profit rather than as ends in themselves, violating Kant's categorical imperative. The practice is unethical regardless of the consequences because it violates the duty to respect persons' autonomy.

rubric.key: Correct application of first ethical framework
Correct application of second ethical framework
Clear ethical conclusions from each framework

major_assignments.title: Business Law and Ethics Case Analysis Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Build a portfolio of four case analyses covering contract law, business organizations, intellectual property, and business ethics. Each case requires a legal analysis using IRAC method and an ethical analysis using at least two frameworks.
objectives: Apply legal reasoning (IRAC method) to real business disputes
Integrate ethical analysis with legal analysis
Develop practical recommendations that are both legal and ethical
Build a professional portfolio demonstrating legal literacy
deliverables: Each analysis includes IRAC legal analysis
Each analysis includes ethical framework application
Reflective summary essay (2 pages) on lessons learned

criteria: IRAC method correctly applied in all cases
Relevant laws and statutes accurately cited
Legal reasoning is logical and well-supported
Alternative legal arguments considered
At least two ethical frameworks applied per case
Frameworks correctly applied to facts
Ethical conclusions are thoughtful and nuanced
Tension between competing ethical perspectives acknowledged
Practical and implementable recommendations
Balance legal compliance with ethical obligation
Consider business impact of recommendations
Preventive measures for future situations
Professional writing and formatting
Consistent quality across all four analyses
Reflective essay demonstrates growth
Proper legal and academic citations
Rigorous legal analysis using IRAC across all cases
Thoughtful ethical analysis with multiple frameworks
Practical recommendations balancing law and ethics
Professional portfolio quality with reflective growth

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of legal concepts and ethical reasoning

class_participation.description: Quality of contributions to case discussions and ethics debates

midterm_examination.description: Covers contract law, business organizations, and intellectual property

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including ethics, compliance, and regulatory law

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Ethics debate participation is integral to the course grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Case analyses penalized 10% per day. Portfolio must be submitted complete; incomplete portfolios receive a maximum of 50%.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be individual. Discussion of cases in study groups is encouraged but written analysis must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Legal citations must be accurate. Fabricating case law or misrepresenting holdings is treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to legal databases through university library. Familiarity with proper legal citation format.
